text: Aziz Beck, I have a paid community
helping students getting into corporate
companies. At the moment, it's $39 a
month. An average that landing the ideal
job in 60 days. How can I retain them
longer after they've achieved the
desired outcome?
>> Really good question. So,
you have a really defined outcome
and so I would say that what's the next
problem that someone's going to want to
solve? So, in all likelihood, like I'll
say this one is you could build an
entire business off of just getting
people into roles. I mean, that's
fundamentally what the entire recruiting
and placement industry is based on. So,
I don't see that as necessarily a bad
business overall. There's billion-dollar
companies that just do that. Now you'll
also note that most of these companies
niche down to one particular type role
and then they o over time they build up
a big network of engineers. They build a
big network of sales people. They build
a big network of customer success.
Whatever the niche is that they're have
for legal interns for whatever, right?
But after they build that the if you
wanted to have something that was
ongoing, it would probably be some sort
of like com like professional
association that'd be a big fraction of
the price uh that would talk about
career advancement and continued
education. And again, this is really
only possible if you have one specific
niche that you're going after. And so
I'll use the engineers example we had
earlier. If you help someone get the
role, then what once they have the role,
their next problem to solve is going to
be their upper trajectory in the
business. And so helping them get
raises, helping them uplevel their
skills so that they can earn more,
helping them negotiate, you know, their
annual raise or whatever. And then also
help helping them use that to bid out
other roles at other companies to move
up. And so it depends on really what you
sell on the front end, which is like you
could just sell the foot in the door,
which is one way. But if you paint a
larger vision of this is a career for
you and the the five-year playbook is
this, you could keep people much longer
if you basically say, "Cool, step one is
you get your foot in the door." And
that's why we have our foot in the door
program that gets you from not making
money as an engineer to being an
engineer. But with engineers, for
example, like you can make millions of
dollars a year as an engineer if you're
really good. And so what we want to do
is we walk you through our strategic,
you know, program where you're going to
learn these three languages, which are
the highest demand languages that pay
the most. And you're going to do those
over the next 12 months. After that
period, we're going to go through the
another job cycle. We're going to take
your new resume, new skill set. We're
going to bid it out. And there's two
outcomes. One is that you get a job that
you like. The alternative is that you
get an offer. You can go back to your
original employer and say, "Hey, I've
upleveled these skills. The other
companies value me at this price. Are
you willing to match it?" And so that's
another way that you can just get a
raise within the existing role. And so
it really depends on what what your
skill set is that you can teach. And
that was an engineering example, but you
could use the same thing with legal. If
there's different types of law that
someone could learn, tax law, whatever,
that's more valuable than generic law.
Um, but all of this is really enabled by
niching down enough that you can really
know the problems of the specific avatar
and provide very high value solutions.
